Beginning of one of many great notes

title: Style Showcase
text:
This post exists purely to stress-test styling: live Python code blocks, collapsible callouts, and margin sidenotes.1
1 Sidenotes render in the right margin when reference-location: margin is set and the viewport is wide enough. On narrow screens they fall back to footnotes at the bottom.

title: Style Showcase
text:
Plain note






Note



This is a plain, always-visible note callout. It is useful for asides that every reader should see.

title: Style Showcase
text:
Collapsible tip






TipClick to expand: numerical stability trick





When computing \(\log\sum_i e^{x_i}\), subtract the max first:
\[\log\sum_i e^{x_i} = x_{\max} + \log\sum_i e^{x_i - x_{\max}}\]
This is the log-sum-exp trick and avoids floating-point overflow.3



3 The same trick appears in softmax computation: divide numerator and denominator by \(e^{x_{\max}}\), which leaves the result unchanged but keeps all exponents non-positive.

title: Style Showcase
text:
Collapsible warning






WarningClick to expand: watch out for default RNG state





Calling np.random.seed(42) sets the legacy global RNG. Prefer np.random.default_rng(42) which returns an isolated Generator object and does not touch global state.

title: Style Showcase
text:
Collapsible caution






CautionClick to expand: matplotlib backend





plt.show() behaves differently in scripts vs. notebooks vs. Pyodide. In Pyodide the inline backend is active by default, so plt.show() renders the figure inline without extra setup.

title: Style Showcase
text:
This note exists purely to stress-test styling: live Python cells, collapsible callouts, and margin sidenotes.1
1 Notes differ from blog posts mainly in intent — a note is a running reference document while a blog post is a narrative piece. Both share the same Quarto machinery here.

title: Style Showcase
text:
Definition (always visible)






Note



Multivariate Normal. \(X \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu, \Sigma)\) has density
\[p(x) = \frac{1}{(2\pi)^{d/2}|\Sigma|^{1/2}} \exp\!\left(-\tfrac{1}{2}(x-\mu)^\top \Sigma^{-1}(x-\mu)\right).\]

title: Style Showcase
text:
Derivation (collapsed)






TipClick to expand: sampling via Cholesky





To draw \(X \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu, \Sigma)\):

Compute the Cholesky factor \(L\) such that \(\Sigma = LL^\top\).
Draw \(Z \sim \mathcal{N}(0, I)\).
Return \(X = \mu + LZ\).

Why it works: \(\text{Cov}(LZ) = L\,\text{Cov}(Z)\,L^\top = LIL^\top = \Sigma\). 2



2 Cholesky is \(O(d^3)\) and only needs to be computed once per covariance matrix, making repeated sampling cheap.

title: Style Showcase
text:
Common pitfall (collapsed)






WarningClick to expand: \(\Sigma\) must be positive semi-definite





Passing a non-PSD matrix to np.random.multivariate_normal raises LinAlgError. A quick fix is to add a small diagonal jitter: Σ + ε·I.

title: Style Showcase
text:
Implementation note (collapsed)






CautionClick to expand: numerical precision of the log-density





Computing \(\log p(x)\) directly can underflow for high \(d\). Prefer working in log-space throughout:
from scipy.stats import multivariate_normal
logp = multivariate_normal.logpdf(x, mean=mu, cov=Sigma)
